content:
Stop typing the same long command over and over. Give it a nickname instead

3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Lee Campbell on UnsplashYou’ve typed it a hundred times by now.
Some long, specific command you use almost every day. Maybe it’s just ls -la with a few extra flags you always add out of habit.
You type the whole thing, every single time. Nobody ever told you that you don’t have to.
Your terminal lets you give any command a nickname.
Type the nickname, and the full command runs. Two letters can replace fifty characters, permanently, every time you open a new terminal window from now on.
The basic trick
This is called an alias. Here’s the simplest version.
alias ll='ls -la'Type that, hit enter, and from that point in your current session, typing ll runs the full ls -la command instead. Short for a longer one. Two letters instead of seven.
This works for anything. Not just short commands. Long ones too.
alias backup='rsync -avz ~/Documents ~/Backup'Now backup does the whole job. No more typing out flags and folder paths every time…
